original_abstract:
Knowledge graph embedding aims to represent entities and relations in a continuous low-dimensional vector space to facilitate efficient link prediction and reasoning. While TransE has emerged as a state-of-the-art method due to its simplicity and scalability, it struggles to effectively model complex relational properties, such as reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. Conversely, existing models that can capture these complexities often suffer from high computational costs and limited scalability. 

To address these limitations, we propose TransH (Translating on Hyperplanes), which models each relation as a translation operation on a relation-specific hyperplane. This approach allows an entity to have different distributed representations depending on the relation it is involved in, significantly enhancing the model's capacity while maintaining the computational efficiency of TransE. Furthermore, we introduce a new training strategy that utilizes mapping properties to weight the construction of negative samples, thereby reducing the prevalence of false negatives in incomplete knowledge graphs. We evaluate TransH on benchmark datasets including WordNet and Freebase for tasks such as link prediction, triplet classification, and relational fact extraction. Performance results demonstrate that TransH consistently outperforms TransE and other baseline models, offering a superior balance between predictive accuracy and scalability.
humanized_abstract:
Knowledge graph embedding as a novel approach to represent entities and relations in a continuous low-dimensional vector space to make link prediction and reasoning faster and more efficient. While TransE, which is the pioneering work in the KG embedding research, has become the state-of-the-art model because of its simplicity and high efficiency in learning embeddings. It cannot sufficiently describe the complex relational properties such as reflexive relations and non-reflexive directional relations, i.e., one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. While several methods have achieved the ability to model the complex relational properties, they still have the restrictions of high complexity in learning the model and low efficiency in prediction and reasoning.

Abstract In spite of the great success achieved by translational-based models, they suffer from two main shortcomings. First, the vector space mapping has to be limited in order to preserve translational semantics, which constrains the capacity of the model. Second, constructing valid negative samples for evaluating models is particularly difficult in the case of incomplete knowledge graphs, which leads to false negatives and hence lowers the accuracy of models. To tackle these two challenges, in this paper we introduce a new translational-based model, named TransH (Translating on Hyperplanes), which translates an entity into another hyperplane corresponding to a relation, allowing each entity to have different representations corresponding to different relations. With TransH, we are able to greatly increase the capacity of translational-based models while keeping them as efficient as the popular TransE. Moreover, we propose an innovative way to make use of the mapping properties of relations for selecting more effective negative samples, which decreases the number of false negatives in incomplete KGs. We experimentally evaluate the proposed TransH model and other baseline models on WordNet and Freebase datasets for link prediction, triplet classification and relational fact extraction tasks. Experiment results show that our model not only improves the accuracy of baseline models but also achieves better tradeoff between accuracy and efficiency.
